metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the initial digest-seeding step is idempotent (duplicate enqueue is a no-op or de-duplicated at consume time), document the system's behaviour when the digest corpus is partially populated (e.g., graceful degradation warning), and add a rollback note stating that stale sidecar digests are safe to delete without data loss (compensating-forward path).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a backfill of Cupid digests for ALL existing tickets in the store but does not explicitly specify that the initial seeding step is idempotent (re-runnable without double-enqueuing) or how the overlap detector degrades when only a partial fraction of digests have been generated (e.g., returns no candidates, warns, or errors).
findings.location: Design section — 'Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)'; Success Criteria; child stories S1–S7
findings.suggested_fix: In S1 or the ADR, add: (1) a deduplication key on the enrichment queue so re-seeding is idempotent; (2) a coverage metric logged at query time (e.g., '312/500 digests ready — results may be incomplete'); (3) a one-line rollback note: 'Rollback removes the queue consumer and digest writer; existing sidecar files are inert and may be deleted with `rebar digest purge` — no ticket data is mutated. DATA LOSS RISK: none (sidecars are additive).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a retrieval-recall spike: run BM25F on a labeled set of ticket pairs (including jargon-heavy and cross-epic pairs) and measure recall@20 before committing to the ~20-candidate cap and BM25F as the sole retrieval stage.
findings.finding: BM25F retrieval recall at the candidate-narrowing stage (~20 candidates) is asserted, not derived from any spike or measurement on the actual ticket corpus. If BM25F's recall is poor on short, jargon-heavy ticket text, true overlaps are silently dropped before the validated LLM judge ever sees them — and the S6 precision fixture does not cover this retrieval stage.
findings.location: Design section: 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates'
findings.suggested_fix: In S1 or a pre-S1 spike story, build a small labeled fixture of known-overlapping ticket pairs (drawn from the existing store's history of manually linked tickets), run BM25F retrieval, and measure recall@k for k=10,20,30. Record the result in the ADR and let it drive the candidate-cap choice. If recall@20 < 80%, consider expanding k or adding a lightweight embedding fallback for the retrieval stage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign ADR authorship to exactly one child story (or add a dedicated S8 story) with a Scope item 'Create docs/adr/<N>-cupid-overlap.md' and an AC asserting the file exists and covers all four required topics.
findings.finding: No child owns the ADR deliverable (AC4/SC5). The parent requires an ADR under docs/adr/ capturing Cupid-digest-without-embeddings, event-store-as-enrichment-queue, digest-as-REVIEW_RESULT-style-sidecar, and overlap-at-review-time-not-as-5fd2-criterion. ee3d(S1) explicitly defers it to 'epic level,' but no story includes the ADR as a scope item, no child AC verifies its existence, and no test checks for the file. The ADR will not exist after S1–S7 land unless a story owns it.
findings.location: Parent AC4 / SC5; child ee3d-fc7b-e280-4cbb (Docs section)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to one child (0f70/S7 is the natural integration story) a Scope line 'Write docs/adr/<N>-cupid-overlap.md covering Cupid-digest-without-embeddings, event-store-as-enrichment-queue, digest-as-REVIEW_RESULT-style-sidecar, overlap-at-review-time-not-as-5fd2-criterion' and a matching AC: 'ADR exists at docs/adr/ and covers the four required architecture decisions; verified by a test/CI check that the file exists.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to S5 (5a8f) a testing requirement: run the existing next_batch tests (or add a regression fixture) after the parent-map refactor to verify next_batch behavior is byte-identical to pre-refactor.
findings.finding: S5 (5a8f) plans to refactor next_batch.py's BFS parent-map logic into a new shared `related_ticket_ids` utility, changing next_batch.py's internal structure. No child includes a test verifying that next_batch behavior is unchanged after this refactoring, and no child lists next_batch.py as a consumer to be regression-tested.
findings.location: child 5a8f-d6a0-ca32-4f51 (What: 'factor the parent-map build into a shared util used by both... next_batch.py's BFS is epic-scoped, private, and NOT reusable as-is; factor the parent-map build into a shared util')
findings.suggested_fix: In 5a8f's Testing section, add: 'Run existing `tests/unit/test_next_batch.py` (or equivalent) against the refactored shared util to verify next_batch scheduling behavior is unchanged; if no such test exists, add a fixture that asserts next_batch output is identical before and after the BFS extraction.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the positive rationale for BM25F-on-Cupid-digest as the retrieval stage: cite the research finding (from the subagent reports) that digest normalization makes BM25F effective for semantic overlap, or add a recall-focused fixture to S6 that validates the retrieval stage independently from the rerank stage.
findings.finding: The plan states 'no embeddings, no vector DB' as the approach for the retrieval stage and names BM25F as the chosen retrieval mechanism, but gives no positive rationale for why BM25F-on-LLM-digest is the right retrieval choice for semantic overlap detection. BM25F is a term-frequency method: it retrieves lexically similar documents well but misses semantic overlap expressed in different vocabulary (the canonical failure mode for synonym-heavy or domain-specific overlap). The Cupid digest is LLM-extracted specifically to normalize vocabulary — but the plan does not state whether this normalization is sufficient to make BM25F effective, or cite any evidence for this. G6(4) requires a positive rationale for the approach's key mechanism choices.
findings.location: Design section — 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate existing pure-Python BM25/BM25F libraries (`rank_bm25`, `bm25s`, `whoosh`) against the plan's field-weighting requirement, and either adopt one (adding it to the `[agents]` or a new optional extra) or document why a bespoke implementation is necessary.
findings.finding: The plan commits to 'BM25F' retrieval (with field-weighting) as a bespoke implementation without considering or ruling out existing pure-Python BM25/BM25F libraries (e.g. `rank_bm25`, `bm25s`, `whoosh`, `pyterrier`) that already handle field-weighted scoring, IDF, and DL normalization. No library is named in the plan text, the plan-of-record comment, or `pyproject.toml`'s optional extras. Field-weighted BM25F (distinct from plain BM25) has non-trivial per-field normalization requirements that an off-the-shelf library handles correctly by default.
findings.location: Design section — Stage 1 description: 'BM25F "More Like This" of the query ticket's digest against the corpus digests'; plan-of-record comment Stage 1 paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add `rank_bm25>=0.3` or `bm25s>=0.2` to the `[agents]` extra in `pyproject.toml` (they are lightweight, pure-Python, and support field scoring); reference the chosen library in the S5 child story spec. If BM25F's per-field variant is required beyond what these provide, note that in the ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific BM25F library (e.g., `rank_bm25`, `bm25s`) or explicitly commit to a bespoke implementation in the Design section, and add a SPIKE story (or a proof-of-concept note) to confirm field-weighted BM25F recall is adequate on a sample of the ticket corpus before committing S1–S7.
findings.finding: The plan asserts BM25F as the Stage-1 retrieval mechanism ('brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates') but neither names a library that provides BM25F nor confirms a bespoke implementation is planned, and no BM25F code exists anywhere in the codebase. This is an unverified capability for a component the system's recall quality depends on, and no SPIKE is called for before committing to S1–S7.
findings.location: Design section — 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests narrows to ~20 candidates'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sequencing note in S7 (0f70) and S3 (e1f4) stating the relative positions of their edits within `_run_plan_review` (e.g., 'overlap injection at step N; enqueue-on-cert at step N+1') and ensure one story declares a dependency on the other's edit or they are batched in the same implementation task.
findings.finding: S7 (0f70) and S3 (e1f4) both plan edits inside `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` — specifically within or adjacent to `_run_plan_review` — without coordinating edit ordering or ownership. S7 injects overlap findings before attestation signing; S3 appends ENQUEUE_ENRICH in the same locked batch as the certification/attestation write. Both touch the same function's attestation region with no stated merge strategy or sequencing contract between the two stories.
findings.location: child 0f70-6d61-2b78-4c34 (Scope: '_run_plan_review injection'); child e1f4-d518-3240-472e (Scope: 'Enqueue hook in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ certification path')
findings.suggested_fix: In 0f70's Scope, add: 'The overlap injection (before attestation) is sequenced strictly before the ENQUEUE_ENRICH append (inside attestation lock-batch, per e1f4); the two edits must be reviewed together.' In e1f4's Dependencies, add 0f70 or note that its enqueue hook lands after the overlap[] key is written.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the two-stage BM25F+Cupid-digest approach relative to the already-shipped `spec_scan.scan_epics_for_spec` batch-LLM overlap detector: explain why BM25F pre-filtering + pairwise judge is preferred over extending `spec_scan` for the at-review-time cross-ticket use case (e.g., cost at scale, latency, precision on non-epic tickets, the query-ticket-graph exclusion requirement).
findings.finding: The plan does not acknowledge or distinguish its chosen two-stage BM25F+judge approach from the already-shipped `spec_scan.scan_epics_for_spec` / `spec_alignment` reviewer, which already performs batch LLM-based scope-overlap detection across all open epics (see `src/rebar/llm/spec_scan.py` and `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/spec_alignment.md`). That existing mechanism batches all epics against a spec/query in LLM passes and reports scope overlaps — it is structurally different (direct batch-LLM vs. BM25F-then-rerank) but targets the same named problem (semantic overlap between tickets across the store). G6 requires the plan to state a positive rationale for the chosen approach over plausible alternatives; the absence of any acknowledgment of the existing overlap-detection infrastructure leaves an implementer unable to tell whether the new BM25F+digest system is a deliberate improvement over or replacement for `spec_scan`, or an independent parallel system that risks duplication.
findings.location: Context section — 'Existing coverage is partial and different in kind: G4 checks consistency WITHIN one epic's children; next_batch schedules around DETERMINISTIC file-impact conflicts; duplicates/supersedes are MANUAL links'; Design section overall
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section, add a sentence explaining the relationship to `spec_scan`: e.g., 'Unlike `spec_scan` (which evaluates a batch of epics against a written spec and is invoked explicitly), this step runs automatically at plan-review time, must cover non-epic tickets, must exclude the query ticket's own graph, and must scale to a corpus of N tickets without a linear LLM cost — motivating the BM25F pre-filter that `spec_scan` does not use.' If this rationale does not hold, consider extending `spec_scan` instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State concrete measurable thresholds in AC1 and SC3: e.g., 'recall ≥ X% on the S6 labeled fixture' and 'precision ≥ Y% on related-but-distinct pairs in the fixture', so the S6 evaluation has a deterministic pass/fail verdict.
findings.finding: AC1 ('Detects semantic overlap/duplication...delivered across S1–S7') contains no detection-rate or recall threshold, so any implementation that detects even one overlap satisfies it as written. Correspondingly, SC3 states 'a related-but-distinct pair must not be surfaced' but names no numeric precision floor over the labeled fixture — making the criterion effectively pass/fail on one unspecified pair, which is unmeasurable in practice.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1; Success Criteria — SC3 ('Low false-flagging...')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'detects ≥ [N]% of true-duplicate pairs in the S6 labeled fixture'. Add to SC3: 'precision on related-but-distinct fixture pairs ≥ [P]% (suggested default: 80%)'. Even a stated default is better than an open question.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default bound for the LLM pairwise judge in the Design section (e.g., 'at most 20 pairwise calls per review run, one per BM25F candidate; adjust in S2 if latency SLA is exceeded').
findings.finding: The phrase 'bounded LLM pairwise judge' in the Design section leaves the bound (max LLM calls per review run, token budget, or wall-clock ceiling) unspecified, deferring a real cost/latency design constraint to the implementor of S2/S7.
findings.location: Design section — '(2) a bounded LLM pairwise judge classifies each candidate'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'The judge is bounded to at most N pairwise calls per run (default: the full BM25F candidate set, ≤20); if wall-clock exceeds T seconds the step emits a partial result and logs a warning.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a wall-clock SLA (or an explicit 'best-effort, no hard ceiling') for the store-wide overlap step in the Design section so S7 implementors know whether to add a timeout/partial-result fallback.
findings.finding: No latency or wall-clock SLA is stated for the store-wide step running 'during the plan-review window'. Because this step runs synchronously (or near-synchronously) in that window, the acceptable ceiling matters for implementors choosing sync vs. async execution and for operators judging whether it degrades plan-review throughput.
findings.location: Design section — 'It runs as a distinct STORE-WIDE step during the plan-review invocation window (before claim)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Target wall-clock for the retrieval+judge stage: ≤ 30 s; if exceeded, emit whatever candidates were scored so far and surface a warning. This default may be adjusted in S7.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that verifies the labeled fixture (S6) exists, precision is measured against it, and at least one related-but-distinct ticket pair is confirmed not surfaced as a false positive.
findings.finding: SC#3 describes a verifiable deliverable — precision measured on a labeled fixture of ticket pairs (S6), with a specific constraint that a related-but-distinct pair must NOT be surfaced — but no Acceptance Criterion covers this. The labeled fixture's existence, the precision measurement, and the pass/fail threshold are all unverified by any AC.
findings.location: Success Criteria #3 / Acceptance Criteria section (no corresponding entry)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] A labeled fixture of ticket pairs (including at least one related-but-distinct pair) exists under S6; the overlap detector is run against it and the related-but-distinct pair is NOT surfaced as a candidate link (precision gate passes). (S6)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the BM25F candidate cap and the suggestion-surface limit (≤3) are named, configurable parameters (e.g., app config or a constants module), not hardcoded literals, so tuning after S6 measurement does not require a code change.
findings.finding: Key numeric thresholds — BM25F candidate cap (~20) and suggestion limit (≤3) — are stated as literals in the plan with no specification that they will be configurable parameters. As the ticket store grows or tuning reveals better values, changing them requires a code edit rather than a config change.
findings.location: Design section: 'narrows to ~20 candidates'; Acceptance Criteria: 'Surfaces ≤3 candidate... relations'
findings.suggested_fix: Define OVERLAP_CANDIDATE_CAP and OVERLAP_SURFACE_LIMIT as named config values (e.g., in a rebar config file or environment variable), defaulting to 20 and 3 respectively. Reference them by name in the ADR so future tuning is a config edit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete user-facing validation mechanism — e.g., a before/after workflow description (duplicate detected at plan-review vs. at merge/completion), an acceptance rate metric on surfaced suggestions, or a named dogfooding session — so the feature's value to its intended beneficiary (plan reviewers / ticket authors) is verifiable beyond the internal precision fixture.
findings.finding: No concrete validation mechanism exists for the human-facing value: the only quality signal is a precision fixture (S6) that is entirely system-internal. There is no before/after workflow comparison, adoption metric, or dogfooding plan to confirm that surfaced suggestions actually help developers/reviewers avoid duplicate work in practice.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Low false-flagging' bullet; Acceptance Criteria overall
findings.suggested_fix: In the Success Criteria or a new 'Validation' section, add a criterion such as: 'In a dogfooding run over the last N completed tickets, at least one true overlap is detected that was not caught by existing file-impact or manual links, and the surfaced `rebar link` command is confirmed correct by a human reviewer.' This makes the human-facing value observable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify which story (likely the first 1–2 of S1–S7) delivers a complete but minimal end-to-end path — synchronous digest computation + BM25F retrieval + LLM pairwise judge + advisory surface output — so the core loop is validated before the async event-sourced enrichment queue is built.
findings.finding: The plan references S1–S7 as implementing the system but provides no sequencing rationale: it is unclear whether any story delivers a thin end-to-end vertical slice (e.g., on-demand digest + BM25F retrieval + LLM judge + surfaced suggestion) before the full event-sourced enrichment queue infrastructure is built, creating big-bang integration risk across all seven stories.
findings.location: Design section / 'Child stories S1–S7 implement it'
findings.suggested_fix: Designate S1 (or a combined S1+S2 slice) as the MVP that runs digests on-demand (no queue), executes BM25F + LLM judge, and surfaces at least one advisory suggestion end-to-end; gate the event-store enrichment queue story on evidence that the core pipeline precision meets the fixture threshold from S6.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the Cupid digest cache invalidation contract: state what event (material edit, description change, AC change) triggers re-enqueuing a ticket for digest re-extraction, and how the overlap detector handles a ticket whose digest is known-stale at review time (skip with a warning? use stale? block on re-extraction?).
findings.finding: The Cupid digest cache has no stated invalidation contract: when a ticket's description or acceptance criteria change (a material edit), the cached digest becomes semantically stale, and BM25F will retrieve candidates against an outdated summary. The parent plan-review gate solves the analogous staleness problem via a material-fingerprint binding (attest.py uses a content hash to detect drift); no equivalent mechanism is described here. A stale digest silently degrades retrieval precision without any observable signal, which defeats the stated precision goal (Success Criteria #3 / S6 fixture).
findings.location: Design section — 'Two-stage retrieve-then-rerank: (0) each ticket gets a cached, LLM-extracted Cupid digest'; Tier 1 enrichment queue description
findings.suggested_fix: Bind the digest to the ticket's material fingerprint (the same content-hash approach used by the plan-review attestation in attest.py). On cache lookup, compare the stored fingerprint against the current ticket content; a mismatch flags the digest as stale and triggers re-extraction (or skips that ticket from the BM25F corpus with a logged caveat). Include the staleness rate in the REVIEW_RESULT-style sidecar so it is observable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the fallback for tickets not yet in the digest corpus: specify whether un-digested tickets are excluded (with a logged coverage-gap count surfaced in the advisory output) or whether BM25F falls back to raw ticket text for those tickets, so recall degradation is observable rather than silent.
findings.finding: The behavior when the digest corpus is partially enriched (some tickets have cached digests, others are still queued or not yet processed) is unspecified. For tickets without a digest, the plan gives no guidance on whether they are silently excluded from BM25F retrieval (a silent miss) or whether the system falls back to raw ticket text. A silent miss means newly-created or recently-edited tickets — precisely those most likely to overlap with the ticket under review — may be invisible to the detector.
findings.location: Design section — 'Enrichment is async via an event-sourced queue drained opportunistically (Tier 1)'; BM25F step description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete closed relation enum for the judge output schema (all six `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` values: `blocks`, `depends_on`, `relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `discovered_from`), and explicitly specify which subset the judge may emit and any mapping/exclusion rules for the remaining values.
findings.finding: The judge output schema's relation enum is structurally incomplete: the plan names only three values (`duplicates`, `supersedes`, `depends_on`) but the live `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` frozenset and the `rebar link` help text define exactly six valid relation values (`blocks`, `depends_on`, `relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `discovered_from`). An executor building the pairwise judge's output schema cannot determine whether `relates_to`, `blocks`, and `discovered_from` are forbidden, silently mapped, or included.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 2 / AC line 2: 'Surfaces ≤3 candidate duplicate/supersede/dependency relations'; Design section: 'a bounded LLM pairwise judge classifies each candidate'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Judge output schema' subsection that defines: `relation_type: Literal['duplicates', 'supersedes', 'depends_on', 'relates_to']` (or whichever subset is in-scope) and explicitly states that `blocks` and `discovered_from` are out of scope for overlap detection with a rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the judge's output schema (verdict enum + confidence or rationale field) and co-locate the decision rule that maps verdict values to suggested `rebar link <id1> <id2> <relation>` commands.
findings.finding: The pairwise judge's verdict/classification vocabulary is never defined. The plan states the judge 'classifies each candidate' but provides no closed enum of possible classification outputs (e.g., `{duplicate, supersedes, depends_on, related_but_distinct, no_relation}`). Without a co-located schema specifying both the verdict dimension and how it maps to a suggested `rebar link` relation, the processing rule for the judge's decision is structurally ambiguous.
findings.location: Design section: 'a bounded LLM pairwise judge classifies each candidate and surfaces ≤3 advisory link suggestions'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the Cupid digest schema (field names, types, and which fields BM25F weights and by how much) and co-locate it with the LLM extraction prompt specification in the relevant child story (S1).
findings.finding: The Cupid digest schema — the central data structure consumed by BM25F and produced by the LLM extraction step — is never defined. The plan references 'LLM-extracted Cupid digest' and 'corpus digests' as the BM25F corpus, but provides no field vocabulary (e.g., title, summary, keywords, file_impact, tech_domain). BM25F is a field-weighted variant of BM25 that requires explicitly named fields and per-field weights; without a digest schema, neither the LLM extraction prompt nor the BM25F index configuration can be implemented.
findings.location: Design section: 'each ticket gets a cached, LLM-extracted Cupid digest'; 'brute-force BM25F over corpus digests'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback behavior for each Stage-2 LLM judge failure mode (timeout, parse error, rate-limit, context-overflow): at minimum define whether the step fails open (skip suggestions, log warning) or degrades to Stage-1 BM25F candidates only.
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for a failed LLM pairwise judge call (Stage 2). The plan describes a 'bounded LLM pairwise judge' that runs at plan-review time, but does not define behavior when the LLM call times out, returns a parse error, exceeds context length, or hits a rate limit. Given the system is advisory-only and must 'NOT affect the claim-gate verdict', an unhandled judge failure could either silently suppress all suggestions or surface a confusing error to the reviewer.
findings.location: Design section: 'a bounded LLM pairwise judge classifies each candidate'; AC line 3: 'advisory-only; does NOT affect the claim-gate verdict'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Failure modes' subsection: 'If the Stage-2 judge call fails for any reason (timeout, parse error, API error), the step fails open: zero suggestions are emitted, a structured warning is logged, and the claim-gate verdict is unaffected.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
